ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for miso soup as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage of prepared Japanese soups is limited.

allergens: gluten (barley or wheat in some miso varieties)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of miso soup is approximately 200 g, typically served as a side dish or starter in a Japanese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Miso soup, prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174240)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for prepared miso soup (FDC ID 174240) used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent a standard prepared miso soup with dashi, tofu, and wakame. This is an approved secondary global source applicable to this Japanese dish where no HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for miso soup is available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for miso soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on the low carbohydrate content of miso soup (approximately 3 g per 100 g), the fermented soy base of miso paste (fermented soy foods have GI values in the range 20–40 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy-based foods), and the absence of significant starch. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 3.0 × 2) / 100 = 1.8, confirming negligible glycemic impact. This estimation is consistent with peer-reviewed literature on fermented soy-based soups.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~490 mg per 100 g; ~980 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit miso soup consumption or request reduced-sodium versions, as a single 200 g serving can contribute approximately 43% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Miso soup has a low glycemic index and a negligible glycemic load per serving (~1.8), making it a suitable side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored concurrently.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content offset by low saturated fat and presence of isoflavones from soy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While miso soup is low in saturated fat and contains cardioprotective soy isoflavones, its high sodium content (~980 mg per serving) poses a risk for individuals with cardiovascular disease who must restrict sodium intake; portion control and low-sodium miso variants are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso soup (味噌汁, Misoshiru) is a traditional Japanese soup made by dissolving miso paste — a fermented soybean condiment — into dashi stock, a light broth typically prepared from kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes). Common additions include silken tofu, wakame seaweed, and sliced spring onions. The soup has a warm, savoury, umami-rich flavour with a slightly salty and earthy character derived from the fermented miso. Its texture is thin and broth-like, with soft tofu and tender seaweed providing gentle textural contrast. Miso soup is a staple of Japanese cuisine, served at virtually every traditional Japanese meal — breakfast, lunch, and dinner — as a side dish alongside rice, pickles, and a main protein. It is also commonly served in Japanese restaurants worldwide, including Japanese chain restaurants in Singapore such as Sukiya. Nutritionally, miso soup is low in calories (~25 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and low in carbohydrates, with a low glycemic index (~30) and negligible glycemic load per serving. However, it is notably high in sodium (~490 mg per 100 g), which is a clinically significant consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The fermented soy base provides small amounts of protein, isoflavones, and B vitamins. Miso soup is not suitable for individuals with soy or gluten allergies, as many miso pastes contain barley or wheat.

accompaniment to rice and main dishes
light snack or warm beverage substitute

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 174240), an approved secondary global source, as no HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for prepared miso soup was identified. GI is an analogous estimate based on fermented soy food GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, as no direct GI measurement for miso soup was found. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Sodium content is high (~490 mg per 100 g); this is characteristic of miso-based preparations and is clinically significant. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source; miso soup prepared with plant-based dashi contains no cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for miso soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 3.0 × 2) / 100 = 1.8. Dietary flags: is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false because standard dashi stock contains katsuobushi (dried bonito, a fish product); vegetarian/vegan versions using kombu-only dashi exist but are not the standard preparation. is_halal set to false as standard dashi contains fish products and miso may contain alcohol from fermentation; halal-certified versions exist. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311434 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as best available ontology anchors for miso soup; these are defensible matches based on published FoodOn and FoodEx2 documentation.
